title: APPENDIX — A reference table and a few resources

lines.text: APPENDIX — A reference table and a few resources
For your reference, here are the major checkpoint inhibitors discussed in this lecture, with target, brand name, year of first approval, and the indication that launched them.
Ipilimumab — anti-CTLA-4 — Yervoy — Bristol-Myers Squibb — 2011 — advanced melanoma.
The first checkpoint inhibitor.
High toxicity, durable responses in about 20% of patients.
Pembrolizumab — anti-PD-1 — Keytruda — Merck — 2014 — advanced melanoma.
Now the world's best-selling drug, approved across more than 40 cancer types.
Nivolumab — anti-PD-1 — Opdivo — Bristol-Myers Squibb — 2014 — advanced melanoma.
The second anti-PD-1; broadly similar to pembrolizumab in efficacy and indications.
Atezolizumab — anti-PD-L1 — Tecentriq — Genentech/Roche — 2016 — urothelial carcinoma, then non-small-cell lung cancer.
Durvalumab — anti-PD-L1 — Imfinzi — AstraZeneca — 2017 — urothelial carcinoma; landmark approval in stage III NSCLC consolidation (PACIFIC trial).
Avelumab — anti-PD-L1 — Bavencio — Pfizer/Merck KGaA — 2017 — Merkel cell carcinoma.
Smaller indication footprint than its anti-PD-L1 siblings.
Cemiplimab — anti-PD-1 — Libtayo — Regeneron/Sanofi — 2018 — cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma.
The third major anti-PD-1.
Dostarlimab — anti-PD-1 — Jemperli — GSK — 2021 — endometrial cancer; notable for the rectal cancer complete-response trial.
Relatlimab — anti-LAG-3 — combined with nivolumab as Opdualag — Bristol-Myers Squibb — 2022 — advanced melanoma.
The first non-CTLA-4 non-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor to reach the clinic.
Tislelizumab, toripalimab, sintilimab, and others — anti-PD-1 antibodies developed primarily in China, with growing global approvals.
Increasingly important in the international market and likely to compete on price as biosimilars emerge.
A few suggested further-reading sources for the curious.
Siddhartha Mukherjee's The Emperor of All Maladies (2010) — for the broader cultural history of cancer therapy, though written before the immunotherapy revolution fully arrived.
Charles Graeber's The Breakthrough (2018) — a popular-press account specifically of the checkpoint-inhibitor story, with substantial material on Allison and on the early melanoma trials.

The 2018 Nobel Prize lectures by James Allison and Tasuku Honjo, available free online at nobelprize.org, are excellent primary sources and convey both the science and the personalities behind the discoveries.

For technical depth, the periodic Hallmarks-of-Cancer review papers by Hanahan and Weinberg, particularly the 2022 update incorporating "unlocking phenotypic plasticity" and "polymorphic microbiomes," provide an integrated framework into which immunotherapy fits.

And for the current state of the field, NCCN guidelines, the major oncology society publications (ASCO, ESMO), and clinicaltrials.gov searches for specific cancer types will give you up-to-date information on standards of care and active investigations.
That brings us to the end.
Thank you for your attention through what has been, by any measure, a dense couple of hours.
If you remember nothing else from today, remember the scaffold:
The immune system has brakes.
Cancer presses them.
We can release them.
That release sometimes cures cancers that were previously incurable.
The brakes are CTLA-4 and PD-1, mostly.
The drugs are the "-mab" antibodies.
The biomarkers are PD-L1, TMB, and MSI.
The dark side is the immune-related adverse events.
The frontier is combinations, vaccines, and the cold-tumor problem.
That's the lecture, in five sentences.
The rest is detail.
But the detail — as you have hopefully come to feel — is where the magic, and the medicine, lives.
Thank you.
